rationale: In the theory development, the citing paper uses Cutright, Bettman, and Fitzsimons (2013) to support a key premise that perceived control affects how consumers draw category boundaries. It explicitly connects its prediction about necessity versus nonnecessity reclassification under stress to that prior control-based boundary finding, so the citation helps shape hypothesis framing rather than serving as a passing mention. The citing paper does not reuse the cited paper's brand-extension context or full mechanism, but it meaningfully transfers the core idea into its own stress-and-spending framework.
relation_description: The citing paper applies the cited paper's control-based category-boundary logic to a different consumer domain. Specifically, it uses prior evidence from brand extension judgments to argue that stress-related control loss can shift what consumers treat as necessities, affecting saving and spending decisions.
